_about: Lexicon for 'A Blind Glass'. Tag a word in a passage with [[word]]; give it a different entry id with [[visible text|id]]. Ids match lower-cased. Type is 'concept' or 'verb'. Use … for elision. A passage with an empty title continues under the previous heading.

makes.s: Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics VI.4, 1140a; Vico, De Antiquissima Sapientia
makes.g: “Making is different from doing” (Nicomachean Ethics 1140a, trans. Rackham). For Aristotle, poiēsis, whose end is a product, is contrasted to praxis, whose end is the act itself. The cause of the made thing lies in the maker, not the made. Behind Stein’s refrain ‘all this makes —’, is the maker’s art. Also see Vico, De Antiquissima Sapientia, where he talks about “the maker’s knowledge”; true learning is what is made with the hands.

system.g: For Aristotle, knowledge is not a heap of facts but a system (systēma, a ‘standing-together’): each science a demonstrative order rising from its own first principles, and inquiry as a whole divided into the theoretical, the practical, and the productive. To understand a thing is to see where it stands within that ordered whole. The idea returns, transformed, after Kant (see Kreines 2024). Stein keeps the word and lets the order spread.

resembling.s: Hume, A Treatise of Human Nature 1.3.1
resembling.g: “It appears, therefore, that of these seven philosophical relations, there remain only four, which depending solely upon ideas, can be the objects of knowledge and certainty. These four are resemblance, contrariety, degrees in quality, and proportions in quantity or number” (Hume, T 1.3.1.2, SBN 70).

difference.g: “But what do we mean by these words, ‘the same’ and ‘other,’ which we have just used? Are they two new classes, different from the other three, but always of necessity mingled with them…” (Sophist 254e). Sameness (tauton) and difference (heteron) are among the ‘greatest kinds’ that let one say a thing differs from another at all.

interpretation.g: “Spoken words are the symbols of mental experience and written words are the symbols of spoken words” (On Interpretation 16a3, trans. Edghill). The opening of the treatise on how sounds come to bear truth and falsity.

believe.g: For Aristotle, knowledge is superior to belief (doxa): “he who has beliefs is, in comparison with the man who knows, not in a healthy state as far as the truth is concerned” (Metaphysics Γ.4, 1008b, trans. Tredennick). As Fred Miller notes, the Nicomachean Ethics (VI.5, 1140b26) characterizes practical wisdom (phronêsis) as an intellectual virtue of the doxastikon — the capacity for belief.

analysis.g: “All instruction given or received by way of argument proceeds from pre-existent knowledge” (Posterior Analytics 71a1, trans. Mure). To analyse is to trace a thing back to the prior premises that demonstrate it.

substance.g: “Substance, in the truest and primary and most definite sense of the word, is that which is neither predicable of a subject nor present in a subject; for instance, the individual man or horse” (Categories 2a11, trans. Edghill). What underlies and persists — yet Stein lets hers be casually ‘replaced.’

sight.g: “This reality, then, that gives their truth to the objects of knowledge and the power of knowing to the knower, you must say is the idea of good… the cause of knowledge, and of truth in so far as known” (Republic 508e, trans. Shorey). Knowing is modelled on seeing; the Good is the sun of the intelligible world. See also Nightingale.

reason.s: Kant, Critique of Pure Reason A51/B75
reason.g: “Thoughts without content are empty, intuitions without concepts are blind” (Critique of Pure Reason, A51/B75). Reason and the senses each need the other before knowledge can arise.

negative.g: “Figure is nothing but determination, and determination is negation” (Letter 50, to Jelles) — omnis determinatio est negatio. For Hegel the negative becomes the engine of the dialectic.

intention.s: Brentano, Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint
intention.g: Brentano revived the Scholastic notion of the “intentional inexistence” of an object: every mental act is “directed upon an object,” containing an object within itself. ‘Aboutness’ becomes the mark of the mental.

necessity.g: “‘Necessary’ means … that without which, as a concomitant condition, life is impossible … Again, what cannot be otherwise we say is necessarily so” (Metaphysics 1015a, trans. Tredennick). Hume later doubts it; Kant relocates it in the mind.

cause.g: “Now there are four senses which are most obvious under which all the causes just described may be classed” (Metaphysics 1013b, trans. Tredennick): the material, formal, efficient, and final.

fact.g: “The ‘facts’ themselves meanwhile are not true. They simply are. Truth is the function of the beliefs that start and terminate among them” (Pragmatism, Lecture VI). The hard particular against which an idea is tested.

actuality.g: “The activity is the end, and the actuality is the activity; hence the term ‘actuality’ is derived from ‘activity,’ and tends to have the meaning of ‘complete reality’” (Metaphysics 1050a, trans. Tredennick).

wondering.g: 'Wonder is the only beginning of philosophy' (Plato). 'It is owing to their wonder that men both now begin and at first began to philosophise' (Aristotle).

decision.g: “The cause of action — the efficient, not the final cause — is choice, and the cause of choice is desire and reasoning directed to some end” (NE 1139a, trans. Rackham). Irwin renders prohairesis as ‘decision’; behind it lies krinein, ‘to cut, to judge,’ the root of ‘crisis’ and Kant’s Kritik. Stein makes it ‘the wound in the decision.’

means.g: Frege distinguishes a sign’s sense (Sinn) from its reference (Bedeutung): ‘morning star’ and ‘evening star’ share a reference but differ in sense. Sense is the mode of presentation — and Stein’s ‘means’ rarely arrives at its reference.

feeling.s: William James, The Principles of Psychology
feeling.g: “Consciousness … does not appear to itself chopped up in bits … It is nothing jointed; it flows. A ‘river’ or a ‘stream’ are the metaphors by which it is most naturally described” (Principles, ch. IX). Feeling as the felt continuity of experience.

recognition.g: “Self-consciousness exists in itself and for itself, in that, and by the fact that it exists for another self-consciousness; that is to say, it is only by being acknowledged or ‘recognized’” (Phenomenology, trans. Baillie). The self won only through the other.

recurrence.g: “What if a demon crept after thee into thy loneliest loneliness … and said to thee: ‘This life, as thou livest it at present, and hast lived it, thou must live it once more, and also innumerable times’” (The Joyful Wisdom §341, trans. Common). To will every moment as returning, identically, forever.

division.g: “That of dividing things again by classes, where the natural joints are, and not trying to break any part, after the manner of a bad carver” (Phaedrus 265e, trans. Fowler). To define is to carve a kind at its joints.

consider.g: “Look round at the courses of the stars, as if thou wert going along with them; and constantly consider the changes of the elements into one another; for such thoughts purge away the filth of the terrene life” (Meditations VII.47, trans. Long). To consider — Latin con-sidus — is to take the measure of a thing under the stars.

description.g: A phrase can mean without naming: ‘the present King of France’ denotes nothing, yet ‘the present King of France is bald’ is not nonsense — it is simply false. Since he is neither bald nor not-bald, “Hegelians, who love a synthesis, will probably conclude that he wears a wig” (On Denoting). Stein: ‘a description is not a birthday.’

reduction.s: Democritus, fr. B125 (in Sextus Empiricus); Mach (1883)
reduction.g: “By convention sweet, by convention bitter, by convention hot, by convention cold, by convention colour: in reality atoms and void” (Democritus, DK B125) — ἄτομα καὶ κενόν. The original reductive thesis, later echoed in Mach’s ‘economy of science.’ Stein resists it: a reduced description is what a description is not.

order.g: “He brought it into order out of disorder, deeming that the former state is in all ways better than the latter” (Timaeus 30a, trans. Bury). The world as an ordered whole.

succession.g: The self as “nothing but a bundle or collection of different perceptions, which succeed each other with an inconceivable rapidity, and are in a perpetual flux and movement” (Treatise 1.4.6). Identity dissolved into succession.

quantity.g: “Quantity is either discrete or continuous” (Categories 4b20, trans. Edghill). How much, as against what kind; in Hegel, the leap from quantity into quality.

certainty.g: “This proposition, I am, I exist, is necessarily true whenever it is put forward by me or conceived in my mind” (Meditations II). The one thing the method of doubt cannot dissolve.

subject.g: The hypokeimenon, the ‘underlying thing’: “the substrate is that of which the rest are predicated, while it is not itself predicated of anything else” (Metaphysics 1028b, trans. Tredennick, who renders it ‘substrate’). Later the word turns inward — Descartes’ and Kant’s thinking subject. Stein puns: ‘not a subject.’

receptacle.g: “…that it should be the receptacle, and as it were the nurse, of all Becoming” (Timaeus 49a, trans. Bury) — later named chōra, space (52a). The formless ground that receives the Forms.

symbol.g: “A sign is a thing which, over and above the impression it makes on the senses, causes something else to come into the mind as a consequence of itself” (On Christian Doctrine II.1) — smoke for fire, a footprint for the animal. The Greek sēmeion (σημεῖον) becomes Latin signum, and later Peirce’s symbol, the sign that signifies by law.

imitation.g: “This, then, will apply to the maker of tragedies also, if he is an imitator and is in his nature three removes from the king and the truth, as are all other imitators” (Republic 597e, trans. Shorey). Art as a copy of a copy.

guess.s: C. S. Peirce, A Neglected Argument for the Reality of God
guess.g: Abduction: the mind’s knack for leaping to the right hypothesis among countless wrong ones. Peirce calls it, after Galileo, il lume naturale: “Modern science has been builded after the model of Galileo, who founded it on il lume naturale” (A Neglected Argument). Reason’s creative first guess.

act.s: Kant, Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
act.g: “Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law” (Groundwork, 1785). The moral law as pure form — and Stein keeps the form, empties the content.

truth.g: “To say of what is that it is not, or of what is not that it is, is false, while to say of what is that it is, and of what is not that it is not, is true” (Metaphysics 1011b, trans. Tredennick). Aquinas later calls it adaequatio; James, simply what works.

contradiction.g: “It is impossible for the same man to suppose at the same time that the same thing is and is not” (Metaphysics 1005b, trans. Tredennick) — the firmest principle of all. For Hegel, by contrast, contradiction becomes the engine of movement. Note Stein’s ‘both sides and the centre.’

harmony.g: “Men do not know how what is at variance agrees with itself. It is an attunement of opposite tensions, like that of the bow and the lyre” (Heraclitus, fr. 51, trans. Burnet). The fitting-together of opposites; for Leibniz, a harmony pre-established among substances.

motion.g: “The fulfilment of what exists potentially, in so far as it exists potentially, is motion” (Physics 201a, trans. Hardie & Gaye). Change as the actualizing of the potential; Zeno tried to argue it impossible.

repetition.g: “Repetition and recollection are the same movement, except in opposite directions” (trans. Hong) — what is recollected is repeated backward; genuine repetition is recollected forward. To take a thing again is not to recall it but to renew it.

suppose.g: The method of hypothesis: “I assume in each case some principle which I consider strongest, and whatever seems to me to agree with this … I regard as true, and whatever disagrees with it, as untrue” (Phaedo 100a, trans. Fowler). To suppose is to posit a ground ‘placed under’ the argument — Latin supponere, the exact twin of Greek hypotithenai; and in medieval logic the doctrine of suppositio asked what a term stands for in a proposition (Ockham, Buridan). The master verb of the book.

measure.g: “Man is the measure of all things: of the things that are, that they are, and of the things that are not, that they are not” — Protagoras’ dictum, reported at Theaetetus 152a. Truth made relative to the measurer.

shows.g: “By demonstration I mean a syllogism productive of scientific knowledge” (Posterior Analytics 71b, trans. Mure). Proof that exhibits why a thing must be so. Stein keeps the showing, withholds the proof.

religion.s: William James, The Varieties of Religious Experience
religion.g: “The feelings, acts, and experiences of individual men in their solitude, so far as they apprehend themselves to stand in relation to whatever they may consider the divine” (Varieties, Lecture II). Religion taken first-personally, before any church.

singularity.g: Scotus’s haecceitas — literally ‘thisness’: the individuating principle, wholly without shared content, by which a thing is not merely human or equine but just this one and no other. The ground of the singular.

clearness.s: Peirce, ‘How to Make Our Ideas Clear’
clearness.g: Descartes’ ‘clear and distinct’ idea, sharpened by Peirce into the pragmatic maxim: “Consider what effects, that might conceivably have practical bearings, we conceive the object of our conception to have. Then, our conception of these effects is the whole of our conception of the object” (1878).

suffering.s: Schopenhauer, The World as Will and Idea, §57
suffering.g: Willing springs from lack, so to will is to suffer; and when desire is sated, “its life swings like a pendulum backwards and forwards between pain and ennui” (§57, trans. Haldane & Kemp). All life is suffering.

legality.g: Kant distinguishes an action’s mere conformity with the law — irrespective of the incentive behind it — which he calls its legality, from its morality, where the idea of duty arising from the law is itself the incentive (Metaphysics of Morals, Introduction). Outward law-abidingness versus acting from duty.

name.g: “Everything has a right name of its own, which comes by nature … there is a kind of inherent correctness in names” (Cratylus 383a, trans. Fowler). Plato sets Cratylus’ naturalism against Hermogenes’ view that names hold only by agreement. Stein’s treatment of names is slippery; the titles hold the proper names of things; the poems underneath often feel disconnected.

education.g: Education “is not in reality what some people proclaim it to be” — not the inserting of sight into blind eyes, but the turning-around (periagoge) of “the entire soul … from the world of becoming … until the soul is able to endure the contemplation … of being” (Republic 518c, trans. Shorey).

giving.g: On how to give: not as an investment expecting return, but freely — “if a gift, it should be given in the way which will be of most service to the receiver” (On Benefits I, trans. Stewart). Here reduced to a bare oscillation.

justice.g: “To do one’s own business and not to be a busybody is justice” (Republic 433b, trans. Shorey). For Plato, justice means each part of soul and city are performing its own work, or alternately the right ordering of the whole.

likeness.g: “To escape is to become like God, so far as this is possible; and to become like God is to become righteous and holy and wise” (Theaetetus 176b, trans. Fowler). Homoiōsis theōi means becoming like the divine.

passages.title: A Carafe, that is a Blind Glass
passages.text: A kind in glass and a cousin, a spectacle and nothing strange a single hurt color and an arrangement in a [[system]] to pointing. All this and not ordinary, not unordered in not [[resembling]]. The [[difference]] is spreading.

passages.title: Glazed Glitter
passages.text: There is no search. But there is, there is that hope and that [[interpretation]] and sometime, surely any is unwelcome, sometime there is breath and there will be a sinecure.

passages.title: A Substance in a Cushion
passages.text: In any kind of place there is a top to covering and it is a pleasure at any rate there is some venturing in refusing to [[believe]] nonsense.

passages.title: A Box
passages.text: So then the order is that a white way of being round is something suggesting a pin and is it disappointing, it is not, it is so rudimentary to be [[analysed|analysis]] and see a fine [[substance]] strangely.

passages.title: A Piece of Coffee
passages.text: The [[sight]] of a [[reason]], the same sight slighter, the sight of a simpler [[negative]] answer, the same sore sounder, the [[intention]] to wishing, the same splendor, the same furniture.

passages.title: A Seltzer Bottle
passages.text: Supposing a certain time selected is assured, suppose it is even [[necessary|necessity]], suppose no other extract is permitted and no more handling is needed.

passages.title: Mildred's Umbrella
passages.text: A [[cause]] and no curve, a cause and loud enough, a cause and extra a loud clash and an extra wagon, a sign of extra.

passages.title: A Chair
passages.text: A [[fact]] is that when the direction is just like that, no more, longer, sudden and at the same time not any sofa, the main action is that without a blaming there is no custody. … To choose it is ended, it is [[actual|actuality]] and more than that it has it certainly has the same treat.

passages.title: More
passages.text: [[Wondering]] so winningly in several kinds of oceans is the reason that makes red so regular and enthusiastic.

passages.title: Malachite
passages.text: The sudden spoon is the same in no size. The sudden spoon is the wound in the [[decision]].

passages.title: A Table
passages.text: A table [[means]] does it not my dear it means a whole steadiness. Is it likely that a change.

passages.title: Roastbeef
passages.text: In the evening there is [[feeling]]. In feeling there is resignation, in feeling there is [[recognition]], in feeling there is [[recurrence]] and entirely mistaken there is pinching.
Any time there is a surface there is a surface and every time there is a [[division]] there is a dividing.
[[Considering|consider]] the circumstances there is no occasion for a reduction, considering that there is no pealing there is no occasion for an obligation.
What is certainly the desertion is not a [[reduced description|reduction]], a [[description]] is not a birthday.
A sound, a whole sound is not separation, a whole sound is in an [[order]].
Claiming nothing, not claiming anything, not a claim in everything, collecting claiming, all this makes a harmony, it even makes a [[succession]].
Sincerely gracious one morning, sincerely graciously trembling, sincere in gracious eloping, all this makes a furnace and a blanket. All this shows [[quantity]].

passages.title: Mutton
passages.text: Interleaved and successive and a sample of smell all this [[makes]] a [[certainty]] a shade.
Light curls very light curls have no more curliness than soup. This is not a [[subject]].
A [[receptacle]] and a [[symbol]] and no monster were present and no more.

passages.title: Breakfast
passages.text: An [[imitation]], more imitation, imitation succeed imitations.

passages.title: Milk
passages.text: Climb up in sight climb in the whole utter needles and a [[guess]] a whole guess is hanging. Hanging hanging.

passages.title: Rooms — the opening
passages.text: [[Act so that|act]] there is no use in a centre. A wide action is not a width.
The instance of there being more is an instance of more. … The [[truth]] has come. There is a disturbance.
There is a [[contradiction]] and naturally returning there comes to be both sides and the centre.
And this was so charming. [[Harmony]] is so essential. Is there pleasure when there is a passage, there is when every room is open.
A silence is not indicated by any [[motion]], less is indicated by a motion, more is not indicated it is enthralled.
Comparing these two comes to be [[repeated|repetition]]. [[Suppose]] they are put together, suppose that there is an interruption.
A [[measure]] is that which put up so that it [[shows]] the length has a steel construction.
A [[religion]], almost a religion, any religion, a quintal in religion … and the [[singularity]], is there a singularity, and the singularity, why is there a question.
This means [[clearness]], it means a regular notion of exercise, it means more than that, it means liking counting.
Why is there more craving than there is in a mountain. … Why is there so much useless [[suffering]]. Why is there.
To consider a lecture, to consider it well is so anxious and so much a charity … will there not be a chance of [[legality]].
Why is the [[name]] changed The name is changed because in the little space there is a tree, in some space there are no trees.
Why is there [[education]], there is education because the two tables which are folding are not tied together with a ribbon.
[[Giving it away|giving]], not giving it away, is there any difference. Giving it away. Not giving it away.
The care with which there is incredible [[justice]] and [[likeness]], all this makes a magnificent asparagus, and also a fountain.
